name: Initiation & Governance
purpose: Establish the foundation of the engagement or product initiative by defining scope, securing sponsorship, aligning stakeholders on objectives, and setting up governance structures. This phase ensures everyone starts with a shared understanding of goals, constraints, and accountability.
commonRisks: Unclear or misaligned stakeholder expectations from the start
Insufficient executive sponsorship or authority
Scope not properly bounded leading to early creep
Key stakeholders not engaged or identified
Governance model not agreed before delivery begins

name: Discovery / Analysis
purpose: Deeply understand user needs, business problems, technical constraints, and market context before committing to solutions. This phase transforms assumptions into validated insights that inform the solution direction and reduce downstream rework.
typicalInputs: Existing system documentation or architecture diagrams
typicalOutputs: Discovery report with key findings and insights
commonRisks: Discovery is rushed or compressed, leading to poor requirement quality
Key user groups or edge cases not represented in research
Assumptions treated as facts without validation
Technical constraints discovered too late in process
Stakeholder buy-in not secured before moving to planning

name: Planning / Backlog
purpose: Translate discovery insights and requirements into a prioritized, sequenced delivery plan and backlog that the team can execute against. This phase establishes the delivery rhythm, resource assignments, and baseline expectations for scope and timeline.
typicalInputs: Dependency map from other teams or systems
typicalOutputs: Definition of Ready and Definition of Done
commonRisks: Backlog items lack sufficient detail for development to begin
Estimates are too optimistic given team velocity and constraints
Dependencies on other teams not factored into the plan
Stakeholders not aligned on scope trade-offs
Technical risks not included as backlog items

name: Design / Architecture
purpose: Define the technical architecture, system design, and user experience patterns that will guide development. This phase ensures the team has shared, documented decisions about how the solution will be structured before significant development investment begins.
typicalInputs: Existing system architecture and integration points
typicalOutputs: Data model and API design specifications
commonRisks: Architecture decisions made without validating key technical assumptions
Design phase skipped or compressed, leading to costly rework
Non-functional requirements not addressed in design
Security and compliance considerations missed
Design not reviewed by or communicated to the delivery team

name: Development
purpose: Build the product or solution features according to designs, architecture, and acceptance criteria through iterative sprints. This phase is where the team converts backlog items into working, tested software through disciplined engineering practices.
typicalInputs: Prioritized sprint backlog with ready stories
commonRisks: Story definitions unclear or incomplete, blocking development
Technical debt accumulating faster than it is paid down
Integration issues not discovered until late in development
Scope creep from mid-sprint changes eroding velocity
Team blocked by external dependencies or environments

name: Testing
purpose: Validate that built features meet acceptance criteria, perform as required, and are free of critical defects before release. This phase encompasses unit testing, integration testing, performance testing, security testing, and user acceptance testing (UAT).
typicalInputs: Acceptance criteria and definition of done
Non-functional requirements for performance and security
commonRisks: Testing starts too late or is compressed at the end of the project
Test environments not representative of production
UAT participants unavailable or not engaged
Critical defects discovered too close to release date
Regression coverage insufficient for safe refactoring

name: CI/CD & Deployment
purpose: Automate and govern the build, test, and deployment pipeline to enable safe, repeatable, and fast delivery of software to production and pre-production environments. This phase ensures deployment processes are reliable, auditable, and reduce the risk of release failures.
typicalInputs: Change management and release approval process
Infrastructure as code and environment scripts
commonRisks: Manual deployment steps introduce human error
Pipeline failures not caught early in the development cycle
Environment configuration drift between dev, staging, and production
Insufficient rollback capability for failed deployments
Change advisory board (CAB) approval delays release

name: Release & Customer Engagement
purpose: Deliver the product or feature to end users through a coordinated release process, including communication, training, and feedback collection. This phase ensures customers are informed, enabled, and supported through the transition to the new solution.
typicalOutputs: Initial customer feedback and NPS data
commonRisks: Customer communication not coordinated with release timing
Support team not adequately prepared for increased volume
Training materials outdated or insufficient for new functionality
Feature adoption lower than expected due to poor change management
Negative customer feedback not captured or actioned quickly

name: Operations
purpose: Monitor, maintain, and optimize the product or system in production to ensure reliability, performance, and security goals are met. This phase involves ongoing observability, incident management, and continuous improvement of system health and user experience.
commonRisks: Insufficient monitoring leading to slow incident detection
No documented runbooks for common failure scenarios
On-call rotation burnout from excessive alert noise
Technical debt causing increasing operational instability
Security vulnerabilities not patched in a timely manner

name: Closure & Continuous Improvement
purpose: Formally close the project or iteration cycle by capturing lessons learned, celebrating outcomes, archiving project artifacts, and identifying improvements for the next cycle. This phase institutionalizes organizational learning and ensures teams benefit from past experiences.
typicalInputs: Team performance data and velocity history
commonRisks: Lessons learned captured but never actioned or institutionalized
Closure rushed, leaving important artifacts unarchived
Team recognition overlooked, harming morale and retention
Retrospective dominated by negatives without celebrating wins
Knowledge not transferred before team disbandment
recommendedPlaybooks: Lessons Learned Capture and Action Playbook
